title: Cat Hip Clicking: Causes, Triage, and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your cat's hip clicking? Learn about joint crepitus, why it's usually low urgency, and how photos and video can help your vet diagnose the issue.
content_html:
TL;DR: Audible hip clicking in cats is often a benign joint sound, but you should consult a vet if it's paired with limping or pain. Providing a video of the clicking can help your veterinarian differentiate between a harmless occurrence and a serious orthopedic condition.

Why does my cat's hip make a clicking or popping sound when they walk?

  • Audible clicking, popping, or snapping sounds from a cat's hip joint are often referred to as crepitus.
  • This sound can be caused by gas bubbles shifting in joint fluid or tendons moving over bony prominences.
  • It may also result from the rubbing of cartilage in a joint affected by age or wear.
  • While unsettling, it is a relatively common occurrence, especially as felines enter their senior years.

Is it an emergency if I hear my cat's hip clicking?

  • The urgency level for an audible hip click, on its own, is generally low.
  • If your cat is moving comfortably and eating well, you can typically wait for a routine veterinary appointment.
  • Seek more immediate care if the clicking is accompanied by a sudden limp or vocalization of pain when touched.
  • Increase urgency if your cat shows a refusal to jump or other signs of distress.

How can a photo or video help my veterinarian diagnose the hip clicking?

  • Capturing a video allows your veterinarian to observe the cat's natural gait without the influence of "vet office adrenaline."
  • A photo of their resting posture can help identify alignment issues at home.
  • Seeing the frequency and specific movements that trigger the click helps distinguish between a harmless pop and a significant orthopedic issue.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Hip dysplasia, a bilateral malformation of the hip joint often associated with secondary osteoarthritis, may manifest as abnormal gait in rapidly growing animals. A characteristic "click" may be elicited by rocking the hindquarter, indicating the femoral head popping in or out of the joint. Radiography can confirm the diagnosis.

Chapter: Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1197)

faqs.question: Does a clicking hip always mean my cat is in pain?
faqs.answer: No, clicking is often painless. If your cat isn't limping or withdrawing from touch, they may not be experiencing any significant discomfort.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat aspirin for joint clicking?
faqs.answer: No. Never give human pain relief medications to cats, as many are highly toxic to their systems. Only use medications prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to hip clicking?
faqs.answer: Yes, breeds prone to hip dysplasia, such as Maine Coons and Persians, may exhibit joint sounds more frequently than others.
